exemplar.title: How does increasing the arm length of a robotic catapult from 24.5 cm to 29.5 cm, with 1 cm intervals, affect the range of the object it throws?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does increasing the arm length of a robotic catapult from 24.5 cm to 29.5 cm, with 1 cm intervals, affect the range of the object it throws?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphing are both clear and precise, with consistent significant figures and labels
Communication of processing steps (means, regression, percentage errors) is coherent and well structured
Use of error bars and uncertainty tables shows strong command of quantitative presentation
weaknesses: Uncertainty treatment is oversimplified, relying on instrument resolution without statistical propagation
Some theoretical derivations (mixing circular motion and projectile equations) are unclear or inconsistent
Uncertainties on the trendline (slope/intercept) are not reported
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is clearly in the top band, while consideration of uncertainties and processing quality show solid performance but some omissions. On balance, the exceptional clarity and one strand at band-5 justify elevating to 5 to reflect the high overall quality.

strengths: Research question set in a precise and coherent context linking historical and modern catapult theory
Apparatus list and stepâbyâstep procedure supplied clear, quantitative detail enabling reproducibility
Methodological considerations are thoroughly explained, with rationale for each controlled variable and acknowledgment of limitations
weaknesses: Minor ambiguities remain in how the landing point is marked, which may introduce systematic bias
Procedure could specify verification of catapult base immobility to reduce slippage uncertainty
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the topâband descriptors. The context is specific and fully described, the methodology is reproducible with minimal ambiguity, and considerations are clearly explained. Small procedural clarifications prevent perfection, hence a topâband mark of 6.

strengths: Conclusion restates the positive correlation between arm length and range, referencing both graph and table
Links observed trend to projectileâmotion theory, supporting the hypothesis
weaknesses: Does not quantitatively integrate uncertainties or gradient into the claim
Comparison to theoretical models remains qualitative and does not reconcile model assumptions with deviations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both conclusion strands sit firmly in band-3-4: the conclusion is consistent with analysis but lacks full quantitative justification, and the scientific context comparison is relevant yet not deeply reconciled with theory. Hence a score of 4.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (motor jerkiness, marking error, 2-D assumptions) and links them to error types
Proposes realistic, relevant improvements (touch sensor, motor upgrade, air-resistance calculations) tied to identified errors
weaknesses: Depth of explanation varies; relative impact of each improvement is not fully quantified
Some suggested extensions remain broad rather than concrete sub-investigations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both evaluation strands fall within band-3-4: the student describes specific weaknesses and realistic improvements but stops short of fully explaining their relative impacts. A mid-band score of 4 accurately reflects this performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and placed within a very specific context: optimising the arm length of a self-built VEX robotic catapult.  The student links the problem to historical catapults, modern robotics, and projectile-motion theory, giving enough background for the reader to understand why the investigation is relevant and appropriate.  Because the context is precise (a particular device, particular range of arm lengths and a defined outcome variable) and fully described, the work aligns best with the 5-6 descriptor; a mid-band mark of 5 is justified because, while specific, the context is not exceptionally rich in real-world application beyond the classroom project.

Instrument uncertainties are stated, percentage uncertainties calculated for each mean, and example propagation is shown for theoretical calculations.  However, uncertainty in the averaged range is treated simplistically (instrument precision only, no statistical spread), and error bars on the graph appear identical for all points.  These omissions prevent the work from being âappropriateâ throughout but still show more than limited consideration, fitting band 3-4; the stronger end (4) is chosen.

Methodological considerations are not just listed but **explained**: each controlled variable includes a physical rationale and a concrete control method; qualitative observations discuss jerky motor motion and projectile-angle deviations; limitations such as air resistance and two-dimensional assumptions are acknowledged.  This meets the âexplains relative considerationsâ element of the 5-6 band, though the depth of explanation is uneven, so a score of 5 is awarded rather than 6.

Specific weaknessesâjerky motor motion, human marking error, unintended launch angles, 2-D assumptionâare *described* and linked to their impact (random vs systematic, high percentage error).  The relative influence is suggested (e.g., systematic error dominates), but the depth of explanation is variable.  This matches the descriptor for band 3-4; the richer discussion merits the upper score of 4.

The apparatus list, controlled-variable table, and step-by-step procedure (IâXI) supply enough quantitative detail (arm-length increments, number of trials, measuring devices with uncertainties, room conditions, launch height, motor setting) for another student to reproduce the experiment without major doubt.  Some minor ambiguities remainâe.g., how the release point was identified or how tape was positionedâso full perfection is lacking, but overall reproducibility is very high; therefore a mark of 5 in the top band is appropriate.

Data processing (averages, linear regression, percentage uncertainty, theoretical calculations, percentage error) is largely correct and leads logically to the results.  Nevertheless, the student misapplies some theory (incorrect mixing of circular-motion and projectile equations) and does not statistically justify the best-fit line or uncertainty on the gradient.  These significant but not overwhelming issues place the work in band 3-4, with a score of 4 to reflect mostly sound but imperfect processing.

Improvements such as using a touch sensor or sand bed, upgrading the motor, enlarging the projectile/bucket, and incorporating air-resistance calculations are realistic and clearly linked to identified errors, satisfying the âdescribedâ criterion in band 3-4.  Some explanations of how each change would quantitatively reduce error are brief, so band 5-6 is not reached; a solid 4 is justified.

Raw-data tables include units, uncertainties and sufficient trials; processed-data tables and graph are properly labelled; the scatter plot uses error bars and an equation of best fit.  Presentation is both clear (readable, logically organised) and precise (significant figures are consistent, symbols defined), satisfying the 5-6 descriptor.  Minor typographical clutter keeps it from perfection, so a 5 is appropriate.

The student compares empirical results with two theoretical models (horizontal and oblique projectile motion) and comments on systematic deviation, demonstrating some engagement with accepted physics.  Yet the comparison is qualitative/percentage-error based and does not reconcile why the theoretical model is mismatched or how assumptions (e.g., air resistance) alter expectations.  This partial but relevant comparison fits the mid-band, with a conservative score of 3.

The conclusion restates the observed direct relationship and references both the graph and processed table as support, making it largely consistent with the analysis.  It also links the trend to projectile-motion reasoning.  However, it glosses over the sizeable systematic error revealed and does not quantitatively integrate uncertainty or gradient into the claim, preventing a fully *justified* conclusion.  Hence the high end of band 3-4 (score 4) is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The derivation merging circularâmotion and projectile equations (Eq. $$R = r\tfrac{\theta}{\Delta T}\sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$) lacks clarity. Verify assumptions and unit consistency when combining these models.
You introduce $$\omega=\tfrac{\theta}{\Delta T}$$ correctly, but you should explain how you measured the angular displacement and timing with your motor to justify this substitution.

The introduction effectively situates the research question by linking Archimedesâ historical catapult designs with a modern VEXâbuilt device, providing a specific and coherent context for the investigation.
Background research describes projectile motion types clearly, but it would be helpful to explicitly connect these concepts to how arm length affects launch height and flight time in your experiment.
The stepâbyâstep procedure is thorough, but in Step I you should specify how you verify that the catapult base truly remains immobile (e.g., measure any slippage after launches).
When aligning the metre scale (Step II), consider using a rightâangle tool to ensure perfect perpendicularity to the catapult base for greater accuracy.
The organization of the raw data into tables with 20 readings per length addresses random error effectively and communicates the procedure clearly.
Your description of the independent variable (arm length with 20 readings per length) is clear and allows reproducibility while minimizing random error.
The controlledâvariable table comprehensively lists each variable, its rationale, and exact control method, greatly enhancing reproducibility.
Define precisely how you locate and mark the objectâs landing point (e.g., vertical alignment of scale, marker tip width) to reduce systematic bias in range measurement.
The Pythagorean calculation for arm geometry (Eq. $$a=\sqrt{x^2-h^2}$$) needs clearer context; explain how this relates to the actual release height measurement.

The errorâpropagation formula in Eq. $$\frac{\Delta R}{R}=\frac{\Delta x}{x}+\dots+\sqrt{\frac{\Delta x}{x}}$$ is not standard; revise to use the rootâsumâsquare method for combined uncertainties.
High theoreticalâvsâexperimental errors (>130%) reveal that key assumptions (no air resistance, perfect horizontal release) break down. Revisit these model assumptions.
The comparison table of theoretical vs. actual ranges is comprehensive and highlights systematic discrepancies clearly.
Raw data tables lack statistical measures (e.g., mean, standard deviation) for each arm length set. Including these would support a more rigorous uncertainty analysis.
The mean calculation formula (Eq. $$\overline{R}=\frac{\sum R_i}{20}$$) is presented clearly and demonstrates solid dataâprocessing communication.

Uncertainty averaging uses only instrument resolution. Instead, calculate the standard error of the mean or propagate individual measurement uncertainties statistically for a more accurate Î\overline{R}.
The percentageâuncertainty table is wellâstructured, making clear how measurement resolution impacts both variables.
When plotting the scatter plot, include uncertainty bars and report the slope and intercept uncertainties so the precision of the trendline analysis is transparent.
Graph interpretation correctly notes a strong linear correlation, but the yâintercept translation to match $$R=r\omega$$ is physically unfounded without reâderivation.
The conclusion succinctly restates the observed positive correlation and ties it to projectileâmotion theory, supporting the initial hypothesis.
Consider using a highâspeed angle sensor or camera to measure the release angle and timing more precisely, which would strengthen your theoretical calculations.
The sourcesâofâerror table on page 12 effectively categorizes each error type and impact, and links to feasible improvements.
Your proposed extensions are interesting but broad; refine them into specific, testable subâinvestigations with clear hypotheses and measurement plans.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
